title: Process String with Special Operations II
performanceTarget: didactic language coverage; existing C++ entry remains the top 1% performance champion
note: Didactic Rust re-solve for language coverage. The existing C++ solution is Pareto-optimal and remains the recommended, speed-extreme, and memory-extreme champion.

variants.languageReason: Rust is selected for this didactic entry because it keeps the champion's native, constant-memory two-pass scan while making byte-level matching, signed 64-bit arithmetic, and ownership explicit. It is not claimed to beat the existing C++ champion on LeetCode constants.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Already implemented and selected as the global recommended, runtime, and memory champion. It has the direct LeetCode signature, native byte scans, and the lowest practical constant-factor profile for this problem.
Considered for low-level byte scanning, but it would not improve the algorithmic or memory profile over the existing C++ champion and is less ergonomic for the platform signature.
Selected for didactic coverage. It preserves native execution and O(1) auxiliary state while expressing the inverse operations clearly with byte-slice pattern matching.

Considered for compiled loops, but runtime metadata and bounds-check behavior are less compelling than C++ for the top-performance champion and less instructive than Rust for this folder's learning objective.
Considered because managed runtimes can scan strings quickly, but UTF-16 storage, object overhead, and runtime startup do not help this ASCII-only, constant-state inverse walk.
Considered for concise code, but interpreter or VM overhead is weaker for a 100000-character hard problem when the global target is top-percentile execution time.

variants.approachJustification: Compute the final virtual length, reject out-of-bounds k, then scan the byte program backward. Undo # by halving length and folding k with modulo, undo % by mirroring k, undo a letter by checking whether it created the current last slot, and undo * by restoring one deleted position.

reasoningSummary: The final string is treated as a virtual object. After computing final length, a valid target index is traced backward through the operation program: duplication folds k into the original half, reversal mirrors it, a letter answers only when it created the current last slot, and a relevant delete is undone by increasing length. This Rust folder is didactic; the C++ sibling remains the performance champion.
